title: Other Key Market Metrics
objectives: Explain what free cash flow reveals that profit does not
Use return on equity (ROE) to compare management effectiveness

vocabulary.definition: The net asset value of a company — total assets minus total liabilities. What shareholders would theoretically receive if the company were liquidated at accounting values.
Share price divided by book value per share. A P/B below 1 means the market values the company below its accounting net assets — unusual for healthy companies.
Cash generated from operations minus capital expenditure. The actual cash a company has available to pay dividends, buy back shares, or invest in growth. Harder to manipulate than reported profit.
Net profit divided by shareholders' equity, expressed as a percentage. Measures how efficiently management uses shareholder capital to generate profit. Higher is better.
Total debt divided by shareholders' equity. Measures financial leverage. High D/E means more reliance on borrowed money — higher risk if earnings fall.

activities.title: Beyond P/E — Other Ways to Value a Company
activities.notePrompt: Write: Why might free cash flow be a more honest measure of company health than reported net profit? Give one example of how a company could report high profit but low free cash flow.
activities.content:
P/E is the most used valuation metric but it has limitations. Here are three additional measures that give a fuller picture.

Price-to-Book (P/B): Compares market value to accounting net assets. Useful for banks and asset-heavy companies. A P/B of 1.5 means you pay $1.50 for every $1 of net assets. Most quality businesses trade above 1× book value because their earnings power exceeds their asset value.

Formula: P/B = Share price ÷ (Total equity ÷ Shares on issue)

Free Cash Flow (FCF): What profit should be but often is not. Profit can be manipulated through accounting choices — depreciation timing, revenue recognition, capitalising vs expensing costs. FCF is cash generated from operations minus capital expenditures. Cash is harder to fake.

Example of divergence: A company builds a new factory for $50 million. Under accounting rules, this might be depreciated over 20 years ($2.5M per year hit to profit). But $50M of cash went out the door immediately. FCF falls by $50M in the year of investment; reported profit only falls by $2.5M. A company can report strong profit while burning cash — FCF exposes this.

Return on Equity (ROE): Measures how effectively management deploys shareholder capital. A ROE of 20% means the company generates $20 of profit for every $100 of equity invested. Higher ROE sustained over many years is a hallmark of quality businesses. Warren Buffett's criteria: consistent ROE above 15%.

activities.title: ROE — Are Management Using Your Money Well?
activities.notePrompt: Calculate ROE for: Company A earns $60M profit on $400M equity. Company B earns $200M profit on $2 billion equity. Which management is using capital more efficiently?
activities.content:
Return on equity (ROE) cuts through company size to ask: for every dollar of shareholders' money, how much profit is being generated?

Formula: ROE = Net profit ÷ Shareholders' equity × 100

NZ examples:
Fisher and Paykel Healthcare: Net profit $310M, equity approximately $1.5B
ROE = $310M ÷ $1,500M × 100 = 20.7% — excellent

Spark NZ: Net profit $408M, equity approximately $2.8B
ROE = $408M ÷ $2,800M × 100 = 14.6% — acceptable for a capital-intensive utility-like business

Mainfreight: Net profit approximately $300M, equity approximately $900M
ROE = $300M ÷ $900M × 100 = 33.3% — outstanding

What ROE tells you:
— ROE above 15% consistently: excellent capital allocation, likely has competitive advantages
— ROE 10–15%: adequate
— ROE below 10%: capital is being used inefficiently — management may not be adding enough value

Caution: ROE can be inflated by high debt (leverage). A company borrowing heavily to fund returns looks more profitable per dollar of equity — but carries more risk. Always check the debt-to-equity ratio alongside ROE.

activities.statement: A company with a Price-to-Book ratio below 1.0 is always an excellent investment because you are buying assets cheaper than their accounting value.
activities.explanation: False. A P/B below 1 means the market values the company below its net accounting assets — but accounting values can be misleading. Assets might be overstated (inventory that cannot be sold, goodwill from bad acquisitions), or the company might have genuinely declining earnings that justify the discount. Banks often trade below book value during financial crises because investors fear their loan books contain bad debts. A below-book price is a signal to investigate further, not an automatic buy.

activities.title: Debt — The Double-Edged Sword
activities.notePrompt: Calculate the debt-to-equity ratio for: Company A has $800M debt and $400M equity. Is this high or low? What would happen to this company if its earnings fell 50%?
activities.content:
Debt amplifies both gains and losses. Understanding a company's debt level is crucial before investing.

Debt-to-equity ratio (D/E) = Total debt ÷ Shareholders' equity

A D/E of 1.0 means the company has $1 of debt for every $1 of equity. A D/E of 3.0 means $3 of debt for every $1 of equity — highly leveraged.

Why debt can be good: Borrowing at 6% to invest in projects generating 15% returns adds real value. Moderate leverage is normal and sensible.

Why debt is dangerous when overdone: Debt requires regular interest and principal payments regardless of how the business is performing. In a downturn, a highly leveraged company may not be able to service its debt — leading to covenant breaches, asset sales at distressed prices, or even bankruptcy.

NZ example — Air New Zealand pre-COVID:
Air NZ carried significant debt to fund aircraft purchases. COVID-19 destroyed revenue overnight. The NZ government had to inject $900 million to prevent bankruptcy. Shareholders were severely diluted. High fixed debt costs made the company highly vulnerable to revenue disruption.

Infratil comparison:
Infratil carries substantial debt (typical for infrastructure companies which have stable, predictable cash flows that can reliably service debt). Their debt is matched to long-term contracted revenue — less risky than cyclical businesses carrying the same leverage.

NZX norms by sector:
Utilities/infrastructure: D/E up to 2.0× — normal and manageable
Manufacturing/logistics: D/E 0.3–0.8× — manageable
Retailers/consumer: D/E ideally below 0.5× — volatile revenues need lower debt
Tech companies: ideally minimal debt — growth funds itself or through equity

questions.question: Mainfreight earns $300 million net profit on shareholders' equity of $900 million. What is its ROE and how does it compare to the 15% benchmark for excellent capital allocation?
options: ROE 3.3% — significantly below benchmark, suggesting poor capital allocation
ROE 15.0% — exactly at benchmark, suggesting adequate but not exceptional performance
ROE 33.3% — well above the 15% benchmark, indicating excellent capital use
ROE 300% — calculated by dividing profit by equity expressed in millions
questions.explanation: $300M ÷ $900M × 100 = 33.3% ROE. This is well above Buffett's 15% threshold for excellent capital allocation. Mainfreight consistently generates high returns on equity because it operates efficiently without excessive capital needs — its asset-light logistics model (using third-party transport and leased facilities where possible) allows high returns on a relatively small equity base. This consistent high ROE is a hallmark of a quality business.

questions.question: A company reports net profit of $80 million but its free cash flow is only $15 million. What might explain this gap?
options: The company has been paying excessive dividends which reduce free cash flow below profit
The company has made large capital expenditures (new factories, equipment) that reduce cash flow but are depreciated slowly over many years in the profit calculation
The company has received large tax refunds that inflate reported profit without generating cash
Free cash flow is always lower than profit due to accounting rules — the gap is not concerning
questions.explanation: Large capital expenditure is the most common reason FCF falls well below net profit. If a company spends $65 million on new equipment in a year (reducing FCF by $65M) but depreciates it over 20 years ($3.25M per year hitting profit), reported profit remains high while FCF is much lower. This is not necessarily bad — it might represent valuable growth investment — but it does mean less cash is available for dividends or buybacks this year.

activities.title: Match the Metric to What It Measures

pairs.zone: How many years of current earnings investors are paying
How efficiently management generates profit from shareholder capital
Actual cash generated after capital expenditure — harder to manipulate than profit
How market value compares to accounting net asset value
How much debt the company carries relative to shareholder equity

activities.explanation: Using multiple metrics together gives a much fuller picture than any single metric. A company might look cheap on P/E but have terrible ROE and high debt. Or it might look expensive on P/E but have exceptional free cash flow growth and a pristine balance sheet. Professional analysts routinely use 5–10 metrics together before forming a view.

activities.statement: High ROE is always good — a company achieving 50% ROE is always an excellent investment regardless of other factors.
activities.explanation: Not always. A very high ROE can be driven by excessive debt rather than operational excellence. If a company borrows heavily ($900M debt on $100M equity), a modest profit of $30M looks like 30% ROE — but the company is extremely leveraged and fragile. Always check D/E alongside ROE. Buffett looks for companies with high ROE AND minimal debt — this combination indicates genuine competitive advantage rather than financial engineering.

activities.question: A company has net profit of $45 million and shareholders' equity of $300 million. What is its Return on Equity as a percentage?
activities.explanation: $45M ÷ $300M × 100 = 15%. This exactly meets Buffett's benchmark of 15% for excellent capital allocation. A company consistently generating 15%+ ROE year after year typically possesses durable competitive advantages — it earns significantly more than its cost of capital, compounding shareholder value over time.

activities.title: The Full Metrics Picture
activities.prompt: Look up any NZ company and try to find: P/E ratio, ROE, and whether the company has significant debt (check its annual report or NZX profile). Write three sentences: what do these metrics together tell you about the quality of this business? Is any single metric misleading without the others?
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — using metrics together is the mark of an analyst, not just a data reader.
